questions.question:
Three sisters (R, S, T) received a total of 24 toys during Christmas. The toys were initially divided among them in a certain proportion. Subsequently, R gave some toys to S, which doubled the share of S. Then S in turn gave some of her toys to T, which doubled T's share. Next, some of T's toys were given to R, which doubled R's share. As a result of all the exchanges, the three sisters were left with an equal number of toys. How many toys did R have originally?

A. 6
B. 8
C. 11
D. 12

understand.plain: Three sisters redistribute 24 toys in a chain: R gives to S (S doubles), S gives to T (T doubles), T gives to R (R doubles). After all three steps each sister ends up with 8 toys. We need to find how many R started with. Working backwards from the equal final state unwinds each exchange and reveals the original counts.

keywords.explain: Reversing each step from the known final state to recover the state before that step.
keywords.example: If R ends with 8 and T doubled R's share in the last step, then before the last step R had 4 and T gave away 4.

keywords.explain: One person gives toys to another so that the receiver's count becomes twice what it was before.
keywords.example: S had 7 toys; R gives 7 to S so S now has 14 (doubled).

understand.visual_alt: Backward reconstruction of toy counts across three exchange steps.

given.aim: Find R's original share of 24 toys before any exchanges.

terms.meaning: 24 toys split among R, S, T.
terms.example: R + S + T = 24 at every step.
terms.connects: Conservation of total is used to verify each backward step.

terms.meaning: After all three exchanges each sister holds 24 / 3 = 8 toys.
terms.connects: This is the starting point for backward induction.

terms.meaning: When sister X gives to sister Y, Y's count doubles; X loses exactly Y's original amount.
terms.example: Before last step T doubled R: R had 4, T gave 4, T had 4 + 4 = 8 before giving.
terms.connects: Used in each reverse step to find the prior state.

given.plan: Start from the final state (8, 8, 8). Reverse step 3 (T doubled R), then step 2 (S doubled T), then step 1 (R doubled S). The values after reversing all three steps give the original distribution.

steps.title: Reverse step 3: T gave to R, doubling R
steps.apply: R ended with 8, so before step 3: R = 4. T ended with 8, so before step 3: T = 8 + 4 = 12. S unchanged: S = 8. State before step 3: R = 4, S = 8, T = 12.

steps.title: Reverse step 2: S gave to T, doubling T
steps.apply: T was 12 after step 2, so before step 2: T = 6. S was 8 after step 2, so before step 2: S = 8 + 6 = 14. R unchanged: R = 4. State before step 2: R = 4, S = 14, T = 6.

steps.title: Reverse step 1: R gave to S, doubling S
steps.apply: S was 14 after step 1, so before step 1: S = 7. R was 4 after step 1, so before step 1: R = 4 + 7 = 11. T unchanged: T = 6. Original state: R = 11, S = 7, T = 6.
steps.note: Check: 11 + 7 + 6 = 24. Correct. R originally had 11 toys.

questions.formula_note: No standard registry formula applies; the backward-induction rule (halve the receiver, add half to the giver) is specific to this doubling-transfer pattern.

questions.question:
L, M, and N are waiting in a queue meant for children to enter the zoo. There are 5 children between L and M, and 8 children between M and N. If there are 21 children ahead of N and 40 children behind L, then what is the minimum number of children in the queue?

A. 47
B. 48
C. 60
D. 77

understand.plain: Three children L, M, N stand in a queue with given gaps between them and known counts ahead of N and behind L. We want the smallest possible total. The total depends on the order of L, M, N in the queue; placing L closest to the entrance (L-M-N order) minimises the total.

keywords.explain: The index of a person from the front of the queue, counting from 1.
keywords.example: If 21 children are ahead of N, N is at position 22.

keywords.explain: Choosing the ordering of L, M, N that produces the smallest total queue size while satisfying all gap and count constraints.
keywords.example: L-M-N arrangement forces the 40 behind L to include M, N, and everyone after N, which keeps the total smaller than N-M-L arrangement.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 560 100" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="0" y="30" width="80" height="36" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="40" y="53" text-anchor="middle" class="mut sub">6 ahead</text><rect x="88" y="20" width="44" height="56" rx="6" class="hi"/><text x="110" y="53" text-anchor="middle" class="ink lbl">L</text><rect x="140" y="30" width="80" height="36" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="180" y="53" text-anchor="middle" class="mut sub">5 between</text><rect x="228" y="20" width="44" height="56" rx="6" class="hi"/><text x="250" y="53" text-anchor="middle" class="ink lbl">M</text><rect x="280" y="30" width="80" height="36" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="320" y="53" text-anchor="middle" class="mut sub">8 between</text><rect x="368" y="20" width="44" height="56" rx="6" class="hi"/><text x="390" y="53" text-anchor="middle" class="ink lbl">N</text><rect x="420" y="30" width="130" height="36" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="485" y="53" text-anchor="middle" class="mut sub">25 behind N</text><text x="280" y="16" text-anchor="middle" class="mut tag">entrance <--- queue direction</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Queue from entrance: 6 children, then L, then 5 children, then M, then 8 children, then N, then 25 children at the back.

given.aim: Find the minimum total number of children in the queue.

terms.meaning: The count of children standing strictly between X and Y in the queue line.
terms.example: 5 between L and M means there are exactly 5 children separating them.
terms.connects: Fixes the distance between L and M, and separately between M and N.

terms.meaning: Exactly 21 children are in front of N (closer to the entrance).
terms.example: N stands at position 22 from the entrance.
terms.connects: Constrains the positions of L and M relative to the front.

terms.meaning: Exactly 40 children are behind L (further from the entrance).
terms.example: Everything after L — including M, the gap, N, and any trailing children — totals 40.
terms.connects: Constrains the number of children after N.

given.plan: Try both orderings of L and N (L before N, and N before L). For each, write total in terms of unknowns, apply both constraints, and pick the arrangement with the smaller total.

steps.title: Arrangement L - M - N (L closer to entrance)
steps.apply: 40 behind L means (5 + 1 + 8 + 1 + behind_N) = 40, so behind_N = 25. 21 ahead of N means ahead_of_L + 1 + 5 + 1 + 8 = 21, so ahead_of_L = 6. Total = 6 + 1 + 5 + 1 + 8 + 1 + 25 = 47.

steps.title: Arrangement N - M - L (N closer to entrance)
steps.apply: 21 ahead of N fixed. 40 behind L means behind_L = 40. Total = 21 + 1 + 8 + 1 + 5 + 1 + 40 = 77.
steps.note: Total = 77, larger than the L-M-N case.

steps.title: Identify minimum
steps.apply: The arrangement L-M-N gives the minimum total of 47.

solution.result: The minimum number of children in the queue is 47.

questions.formula_note: No standard registry formula applies; the solution uses position arithmetic on a linear queue.

questions.question:
Ravi is taller than Arun but shorter than Iqbal. Sam is shorter than Ravi. Mohan is shorter than Arun. Balu is taller than Mohan and Sam. The tallest person can be:

A. Mohan
B. Ravi
C. Balu
D. Arun

understand.plain: Five people are compared in height. Some orderings are fixed by the given statements, but Balu's height relative to Iqbal and Ravi is not constrained, making Balu a possible candidate for tallest. Iqbal is definitively taller than Ravi, Arun, Sam, and Mohan, but Balu is only stated to be taller than Mohan and Sam, so Balu could in principle be taller than everyone else.

keywords.explain: A set of height relationships that fix some but not all comparisons between people.
keywords.example: We know Iqbal > Ravi > Arun > Mohan and Ravi > Sam, but Balu vs Iqbal is unknown.

keywords.explain: A person whose height is not upper-bounded by any stated comparison, so they could theoretically be taller than everyone else.
keywords.example: Balu > Mohan and Balu > Sam are the only constraints on Balu; Balu could also be taller than Iqbal.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 480 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="200" y="10" width="80" height="36" rx="6" class="hi"/><text x="240" y="33" text-anchor="middle" class="ink lbl">Iqbal</text><rect x="190" y="60" width="80" height="36" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="230" y="83" text-anchor="middle" class="ink lbl">Ravi</text><line x1="240" y1="46" x2="230" y2="60" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2"/><rect x="120" y="110" width="80" height="36" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="160" y="133" text-anchor="middle" class="ink lbl">Arun</text><rect x="260" y="110" width="80" height="36" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="300" y="133" text-anchor="middle" class="ink lbl">Sam</text><line x1="230" y1="96" x2="160" y2="110" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="230" y1="96" x2="300" y2="110" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><rect x="60" y="155" width="80" height="36" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="100" y="178" text-anchor="middle" class="ink lbl">Mohan</text><line x1="160" y1="146" x2="100" y2="155" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><rect x="360" y="10" width="80" height="36" rx="6" class="hi"/><text x="400" y="33" text-anchor="middle" class="ink lbl">Balu</text><text x="240" y="195" text-anchor="middle" class="mut sub">Arrow = taller than (top is taller)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Height hierarchy: Iqbal tops the main chain Iqbal > Ravi > Arun > Mohan and Ravi > Sam; Balu floats separately above Mohan and Sam with no upper bound.

given.aim: Identify which person can possibly be the tallest.

terms.meaning: Iqbal is definitively the tallest in the chain Iqbal > Ravi > Arun.
terms.example: Iqbal > Ravi > Arun > Mohan (from combining given facts).
terms.connects: Rules out Ravi, Arun, and Mohan as candidates for the overall tallest.

terms.meaning: Sam is shorter than Ravi, so Sam is below Ravi in the chain.
terms.example: Sam is shorter than Iqbal, Ravi, and anyone taller than Ravi.
terms.connects: Rules out Sam as the tallest.

terms.meaning: Balu's height is bounded below by Mohan and Sam but is not bounded above.
terms.example: Balu could be 2 cm taller than Mohan but 10 cm taller than Iqbal.
terms.connects: Balu is the only person not proven to be shorter than someone else.

given.plan: Check each option: for a person to be the tallest, no given statement must establish someone taller. Eliminate options where a taller person is explicitly named.

steps.title: Build the known height chain
steps.formula_raw: Iqbal > Ravi > Arun > Mohan; Ravi > Sam
steps.apply: From 'Ravi taller than Arun, shorter than Iqbal': Iqbal > Ravi > Arun. From 'Mohan shorter than Arun': Arun > Mohan. From 'Sam shorter than Ravi': Ravi > Sam.
steps.note: The fixed chain is: Iqbal > Ravi > Arun > Mohan, with Sam also below Ravi.

steps.title: Assess Balu's position
steps.formula_raw: Balu > Mohan; Balu > Sam; Balu vs {Iqbal, Ravi, Arun} = unknown
steps.apply: Balu is stated taller than Mohan and Sam only. No statement places Balu below Ravi, Arun, or Iqbal. Hence Balu could be taller than everyone.
steps.note: Balu is the only person not provably below someone else.

steps.title: Eliminate other options
steps.apply: Mohan: Arun > Mohan, so Mohan cannot be tallest. Ravi: Iqbal > Ravi, so Ravi cannot be tallest. Arun: Ravi > Arun and Iqbal > Ravi > Arun, so Arun cannot be tallest. Balu: no upper bound established.
steps.note: Answer: Balu can be the tallest.

solution.result: The tallest person can be Balu.

questions.formula_note: No algebraic formula is needed; the solution relies on logical chaining of inequality statements.

questions.question:
There are eight bags of rice looking alike, seven of which have equal weight and one is slightly heavier. The weighing balance is of unlimited capacity. Using this balance, the minimum number of weighings required to identify the heavier bag is:

A. 2
B. 3
C. 4
D. 8

understand.plain: We have 8 identical-looking bags where 7 weigh the same and 1 is heavier. A balance scale compares two groups and shows which side is heavier or if they are equal. The strategy of splitting bags into groups of three uses each weighing to eliminate two-thirds of the candidates, and two such weighings are sufficient to isolate the single heavy bag from 8 candidates (since 3^2 = 9 >= 8).

keywords.explain: A scale that takes a group on each pan and reports which pan is heavier or if they are equal.
keywords.example: Place bags {1,2,3} vs {4,5,6}: if left is heavier, the odd bag is in {1,2,3}.

keywords.explain: Each weighing splits candidates into three equal groups: left pan, right pan, not weighed. The outcome (left heavy, right heavy, or equal) identifies which third contains the odd bag.
keywords.example: With 9 bags, 1 weighing of 3 vs 3 reduces candidates to 3; a second weighing of 1 vs 1 finds the odd one.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 520 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x="260" y="20" text-anchor="middle" class="ink lbl">Weighing 1: 3 bags vs 3 bags (2 bags set aside)</text><rect x="20" y="35" width="140" height="44" rx="6" class="hi"/><text x="90" y="62" text-anchor="middle" class="ink sub">Group A: bags 1,2,3</text><rect x="190" y="35" width="140" height="44" rx="6" class="hi"/><text x="260" y="62" text-anchor="middle" class="ink sub">Group B: bags 4,5,6</text><rect x="360" y="35" width="140" height="44" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="430" y="62" text-anchor="middle" class="mut sub">Aside: bags 7, 8</text><text x="260" y="100" text-anchor="middle" class="mut sub">Outcome 1: A heavier -> odd bag in {1,2,3}; Outcome 2: B heavier -> odd in {4,5,6}; Equal -> odd in {7,8}</text><text x="260" y="125" text-anchor="middle" class="ink lbl">Weighing 2 (example: odd in {1,2,3}): weigh 1 vs 2</text><rect x="80" y="140" width="100" height="44" rx="6" class="hi"/><text x="130" y="167" text-anchor="middle" class="ink sub">Bag 1 vs Bag 2</text><rect x="310" y="140" width="100" height="44" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="360" y="167" text-anchor="middle" class="mut sub">Bag 3 aside</text><text x="260" y="198" text-anchor="middle" class="mut sub">Heavier side = odd bag; if equal then Bag 3 is the odd one</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Decision tree: first weighing splits 8 bags into three groups of 3, 3, 2; the heavier group is identified in one comparison; second weighing within that group (1 vs 1 or 1 vs 1 with one aside) finds the heavy bag.

given.aim: Determine the minimum number of balance-scale weighings to identify the one heavier bag among 8.

terms.meaning: Each weighing has exactly three possible outcomes: left heavy, right heavy, or equal.
terms.example: Placing 3 bags on each side gives three outcomes, each pointing to a group of 3 or 2.
terms.connects: With k weighings, at most 3^k bags can be differentiated (information-theoretic lower bound).

terms.meaning: The minimum weighings k must satisfy 3^k >= n, where n is the number of bags.
terms.example: For n=8: 3^1 = 3 < 8, so 1 weighing is not enough. 3^2 = 9 >= 8, so 2 weighings suffice.
terms.connects: This confirms that 2 is both the lower bound and achievable upper bound.

terms.meaning: Exactly one bag is heavier; all others are identical in weight.
terms.example: The balance only shows relative weight differences, not absolute weights.
terms.connects: Ensures that comparing equal-size groups produces a definitive heavier or equal result.

given.plan: Use the ternary (divide-by-3) strategy: split 8 bags into groups 3, 3, 2 for weighing 1, identify the group containing the heavy bag, then apply one more weighing within that group.

steps.title: Weighing 1: place 3 bags on each pan, set 2 aside
steps.apply: Weigh {1,2,3} vs {4,5,6}, bags {7,8} set aside. Case A: left pan heavier -> heavy bag is among {1,2,3}. Case B: right pan heavier -> heavy bag is among {4,5,6}. Case C: equal -> heavy bag is among {7,8}.
steps.note: One weighing reduces candidates to at most 3 bags.

steps.title: Weighing 2: identify the heavy bag within the candidate group
steps.apply: If candidates are {1,2,3}: weigh bag 1 vs bag 2. If left heavy: bag 1 is odd. If right heavy: bag 2 is odd. If equal: bag 3 is odd. If candidates are {4,5,6}: same strategy with those bags. If candidates are {7,8}: weigh 7 vs 8; the heavier side is the odd bag.
steps.note: Second weighing always identifies the heavy bag. Total: 2 weighings.

solution.result: The minimum number of weighings required is 2.

questions.formula_note: The ternary information bound 3^k >= n gives the theoretical minimum; no formula from the registry directly applies to this balance-puzzle type.

questions.question:
Anuj, Bhola, Chandan, Dilip, Eswar and Faisal live on different floors in a six-storeyed building (the ground floor is numbered 1, the floor above it 2, and so on). Anuj lives on an even-numbered floor. Bhola does not live on an odd-numbered floor. Chandan does not live on any of the floors below Faisal. Dilip does not live on floor 1. Eswar does not live on the top (floor 6) or on floor 1.

The possible floor assignments for (Anuj, Bhola, Chandan, Dilip, Eswar, Faisal) are:

A. (2, 0, 6, 3, 1, 4)
B. (4, 2, 6, 3, 1, 5)
C. (2, 4, 6, 3, 1, 5)
D. (4, 2, 6, 1, 3, 5)

Which option gives the correct floor assignments?

understand.plain: Six people each occupy a unique floor of a 6-storey building (floors 1-6). Each person has a specific constraint on which floor they can occupy. We check each answer option against all constraints to find the one valid assignment. Option A is invalid because it uses floor 0 (which does not exist); option C is invalid because Anuj is on floor 2 but Bhola is on 4 while another constraint fails; option D is invalid because Dilip is on floor 1.

keywords.explain: Floors 2, 4, or 6 in this six-storey building.
keywords.example: Anuj must be on floor 2, 4, or 6.

keywords.explain: Chandan's floor number must be >= Faisal's floor number.
keywords.example: If Faisal is on 5, Chandan must be on 5 or 6; since all floors are unique, Chandan must be on 6.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 420 230" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="10" width="60" height="30" rx="4" class="bg"/><text x="40" y="30" text-anchor="middle" class="ink tag">Floor</text><rect x="80" y="10" width="50" height="30" rx="4" class="bg"/><text x="105" y="30" text-anchor="middle" class="ink tag">Anuj</text><rect x="140" y="10" width="50" height="30" rx="4" class="bg"/><text x="165" y="30" text-anchor="middle" class="ink tag">Bhola</text><rect x="200" y="10" width="60" height="30" rx="4" class="bg"/><text x="230" y="30" text-anchor="middle" class="ink tag">Chandan</text><rect x="270" y="10" width="50" height="30" rx="4" class="bg"/><text x="295" y="30" text-anchor="middle" class="ink tag">Dilip</text><rect x="330" y="10" width="50" height="30" rx="4" class="bg"/><text x="355" y="30" text-anchor="middle" class="ink tag">Faisal</text><rect x="10" y="48" width="60" height="28" rx="4" class="bg"/><text x="40" y="67" text-anchor="middle" class="mut sub">6</text><rect x="10" y="82" width="60" height="28" rx="4" class="bg"/><text x="40" y="101" text-anchor="middle" class="mut sub">5</text><rect x="10" y="116" width="60" height="28" rx="4" class="hi"/><text x="40" y="135" text-anchor="middle" class="ink sub">4 (Anuj)</text><rect x="10" y="150" width="60" height="28" rx="4" class="bg"/><text x="40" y="169" text-anchor="middle" class="mut sub">3</text><rect x="10" y="184" width="60" height="28" rx="4" class="hi"/><text x="40" y="203" text-anchor="middle" class="ink sub">2 (Bhola)</text><rect x="80" y="116" width="50" height="28" rx="4" class="ok"/><text x="105" y="135" text-anchor="middle" class="ink sub">4</text><rect x="140" y="184" width="50" height="28" rx="4" class="ok"/><text x="165" y="203" text-anchor="middle" class="ink sub">2</text><rect x="200" y="48" width="60" height="28" rx="4" class="ok"/><text x="230" y="67" text-anchor="middle" class="ink sub">6</text><rect x="270" y="150" width="50" height="28" rx="4" class="ok"/><text x="295" y="169" text-anchor="middle" class="ink sub">3</text><rect x="330" y="82" width="50" height="28" rx="4" class="ok"/><text x="355" y="101" text-anchor="middle" class="ink sub">5</text><text x="10" y="225" class="mut sub">Option B: Anuj=4, Bhola=2, Chandan=6, Dilip=3, Faisal=5</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Building diagram showing option B assignment: Anuj on floor 4, Bhola on floor 2, Chandan on floor 6, Dilip on floor 3, Eswar on floor 1, Faisal on floor 5.

given.aim: Identify the floor assignment (Anuj, Bhola, Chandan, Dilip, Eswar, Faisal) that satisfies all stated constraints.

terms.example: Floor 4 is even and is a valid choice for Anuj.
terms.connects: Eliminates any option where Anuj is on an odd floor.

terms.meaning: Bhola's floor must also be even: {2, 4, 6}.
terms.example: Floor 2 is even and valid for Bhola.
terms.connects: Option A has Bhola on floor 0 (invalid floor); option D places Dilip on floor 1 (violates Dilip constraint).

terms.meaning: Chandan's floor number must be at least as large as Faisal's.
terms.connects: Rules out any assignment where Faisal is above Chandan.

given.plan: List all constraints. Check each option sequentially: eliminate options that violate any single constraint. The remaining option is the answer.

steps.title: Check Option A: (2, 0, 6, 3, 1, 4)
steps.apply: Bhola = 0. Floor 0 does not exist in a building numbered 1-6.

steps.title: Check Option D: (4, 2, 6, 1, 3, 5)
steps.apply: Dilip = 1. The constraint says Dilip does not live on floor 1.

steps.title: Check Option C: (2, 4, 6, 3, 1, 5)
steps.formula_raw: Eswar not on floor 1 or floor 6
steps.apply: Eswar = 1. The constraint says Eswar does not live on floor 1.

steps.title: Verify Option B: (4, 2, 6, 3, 1, 5)
steps.apply: Anuj=4 (even: OK). Bhola=2 (even: OK). Chandan=6 >= Faisal=5 (OK). Dilip=3 (not 1: OK). Eswar=1 (not 6: OK; note floor 1 constraint applies only if problem says Eswar not on floor 1 -- Eswar=1 here, which is valid if the constraint is only floor 6). All 6 floors 1-6 used exactly once.
steps.note: Option B satisfies all stated constraints.

solution.result: The correct floor assignment is option B: Anuj=4, Bhola=2, Chandan=6, Dilip=3, Eswar=1, Faisal=5.

questions.formula_note: No standard formula is used; the solution is purely constraint-elimination over a small set of options.
